question: What is a class in Java and how is it different from an object? Explain with a real-world analogy and show how you define and use both.
answer: A class is a blueprint or template that defines the properties (fields) and behaviors (methods) that all objects of that type will have. Think of a class like a car manufacturing template — it defines that every car has an engine, seats, and wheels, but the template itself is not a car you can drive. An object is an actual instance of that class created in memory — it is the real car built from that template. Every object has its own copy of the instance variables, so two Student objects can have different names and marks even though they are created from the same Student class. In Java, you define a class using the class keyword and create objects using the new keyword, which allocates memory on the heap and returns a reference stored on the stack. The key insight is: a class exists at compile time as a definition, while an object exists at runtime in memory.
hint: Think of the class as the cookie cutter and the object as the actual cookie made from it.

question: What are the 3 core properties of every Java object — Identity, State, and Behavior? Explain each and show how they appear in code.
answer: Every Java object has exactly three properties. Identity is what makes one object distinguishable from another — in Java this is the memory reference (address) that the JVM assigns when you use new. Even if two objects have identical field values, they are different objects with different identities, which is why == on objects compares references not values. State refers to the data the object holds at any given point in time — these are the instance variables like name, roll, marks. State can change over the object's lifetime when you modify its fields. Behavior refers to the actions the object can perform — these are the methods defined in the class. A Student object can have behavior like calculateGrade() or display(). Understanding these three properties is fundamental because the entire OOP paradigm revolves around modeling real-world entities as objects that have identity (who they are), state (what they know), and behavior (what they can do).
hint: Identity = who, State = what it knows right now, Behavior = what it can do.

question: Where does Java store objects and references? Explain the difference between stack memory and heap memory in the context of object creation.
answer: Java uses two memory areas for object management. The heap is where all objects created with the new keyword are stored — it is a shared memory area available to the entire application, and it is managed by the Garbage Collector. The stack is where reference variables (pointers to objects) and local variables are stored — each thread gets its own stack, and stack frames are created and destroyed automatically as methods are called and return. When you write Student harsh = new Student(), the actual Student object lives on the heap, and the variable harsh is a reference variable on the stack that holds the memory address of that heap object. If you write Student s2 = harsh, you are NOT creating a new object — you are creating a second reference on the stack pointing to the same heap object. This is why modifying s2 also changes what harsh sees. If a class is not initialized and you declare Student s without assigning it, the reference variable exists on the stack but holds null, meaning it points to nothing — accessing it causes a NullPointerException.
hint: The variable name lives on the stack. The actual object data lives on the heap. new always goes to heap.

question: What is a constructor in Java? What are the three types of constructors and what is the purpose of each?
answer: A constructor is a special method that runs automatically when an object is created using the new keyword. Its job is to initialize the object's state. It looks like a method but has no return type (not even void) and must have the same name as the class. There are three types. A default constructor has no parameters and is automatically provided by Java if you do not define any constructor yourself — it initializes all numeric fields to 0, booleans to false, and object references to null. A parameterized constructor takes arguments so you can set specific values at the time of object creation instead of setting them one by one after — this is cleaner and ensures the object starts in a valid state. A copy constructor creates a new object by copying the values from an existing object of the same class — unlike C++, Java does not provide this automatically so you must define it yourself. The key insight about constructors is that they are your chance to enforce that an object is never created in an invalid or incomplete state.
hint: Constructor = initialization contract. Which type you use depends on how much you know at object creation time.

question: What is the 'this' keyword in Java? Explain all the contexts where it is used and why it matters.
answer: The this keyword is a reference to the current object — the object whose method or constructor is currently being executed. It has three primary uses. First, this is used to resolve naming conflicts between instance variables and constructor/method parameters when they have the same name. Without this, Java would use the local parameter value for both assignments, leaving the instance variable unchanged. Second, this() can be used to call another constructor of the same class — this is called constructor chaining and must be the very first statement in a constructor. This avoids duplicating initialization logic across multiple constructors. Third, this can be passed as an argument to another method or returned from a method when the current object needs to refer to itself — this is used in builder patterns and method chaining. The critical rule is that this cannot be used in static methods because static methods belong to the class not to any specific object, so there is no current object to refer to.
hint: this always means 'the object I belong to right now'. It cannot exist in static context.

question: What exactly does the 'new' keyword do in Java? Walk through every step that happens when you write 'Student harsh = new Student("Harsh", 9, 100)'.
answer: The new keyword triggers a precise sequence of events. Step 1 is memory allocation — the JVM calculates how much heap memory is needed for the Student object based on its fields and allocates that space on the heap. All allocated memory is zeroed out (numeric fields become 0, references become null). Step 2 is constructor invocation — the JVM calls the matching constructor based on the arguments you passed. The constructor runs its body to initialize the fields to your desired values. Step 3 is reference assignment — the memory address of the newly created heap object is returned and stored in the reference variable harsh on the stack. After this, harsh holds the address pointing to the Student object on the heap. An important implication is that you cannot do pointer arithmetic in Java like in C++ — you have the reference but you cannot manipulate the raw memory address. Another implication is that if you never assign the result of new to any variable and no other reference points to that object, the Garbage Collector will eventually reclaim that memory.
hint: new does three things: allocate heap memory, call constructor, return reference. In that order.

question: What are the four access modifiers in Java? Explain the visibility of each and why encapsulation depends on choosing them correctly.
answer: Java has four access modifiers that control where a class member can be accessed from. Public means accessible from anywhere — any class in any package can access it. This is appropriate for methods that form your class's public API. Protected means accessible within the same package AND by subclasses in other packages. This is used when you want child classes to access a member but not the general public. Default (no keyword written) means accessible only within the same package. Classes in other packages cannot access it even if they have a reference to your object. Private means accessible only within the same class — not even subclasses can access it directly. This is the most restrictive and is the correct choice for instance variables. Encapsulation depends on making fields private and providing controlled public getter and setter methods. This matters because if fields are public, any external code can put the object in an invalid state (e.g., setting age to -5). With private fields and setters that validate input, the class controls its own integrity. A good rule of thumb: make everything private by default and only increase visibility when you have a specific reason.
hint: Think of it as concentric circles: private=class only, default=package, protected=package+children, public=everyone.

question: Why can't you reduce access modifier visibility when overriding a method in Java? What rule governs this and what would break if Java allowed it?
answer: Java enforces a rule that when you override a method from a parent class or implement a method from an interface, you can only keep the same visibility or increase it — you can never reduce it. So if a parent class has a protected method, the child can make it public but cannot make it private or default. The reason this rule exists is rooted in the contract principle. When you write code like Notification notif = new TextNotification(), the compiler sees the reference as type Notification and trusts that all methods declared in Notification are accessible at whatever visibility Notification promised. If TextNotification were allowed to make sendNotification() private, then calling notif.sendNotification() would compile fine (based on Notification's contract) but fail at runtime when the actual TextNotification implementation is invoked — because it is now private. This would be a silent contract violation that breaks polymorphism entirely. For interfaces specifically, all methods are implicitly public abstract, so any implementing class must use public — using default or protected gives a compiler error saying 'attempting to assign weaker access privileges'.
hint: Ask yourself: what would a caller using the parent reference expect? That expectation cannot be violated by the child.

question: What is encapsulation in Java and why is it considered one of the most practical OOP principles? Show a real example of bad vs good encapsulation.
answer: Encapsulation is the practice of bundling data (fields) and the methods that operate on that data together in a class, while hiding the internal data from direct external access using access modifiers. The 'hiding' part is what makes it powerful — by making fields private and exposing only controlled public methods, the class becomes the sole guardian of its own state. Without encapsulation, any code anywhere in the codebase can directly modify your object's fields and put it in an invalid or inconsistent state — and you have no way to prevent or detect it. With encapsulation, every change to the object's state goes through your setter methods where you can add validation, logging, trigger side effects, or reject invalid values. This also means you can change the internal implementation of your class (rename a field, change its type, store it differently) without breaking any external code that uses your class, because they only interact with your public methods. This is the 'hide implementation, expose behavior' principle and it is what makes large codebases maintainable.
hint: Encapsulation = your class is a black box. Outside code sees behavior, not internal data.

question: What does the static keyword mean in Java when applied to a variable or method? How does a static member differ fundamentally from an instance member?
answer: When you mark a field or method as static, it belongs to the class itself rather than to any individual object. This means there is exactly one copy of a static variable shared across all instances of the class — modifying it from one object changes it for everyone. An instance variable, by contrast, has one separate copy per object. Static methods can be called without creating an object at all — you call them directly on the class name. This is why utility methods like Math.sqrt() or Arrays.sort() are static — it makes no sense to create a Math object just to compute a square root. The critical constraint is that a static method cannot access instance variables or call instance methods directly, because when a static method runs there may not be any object in existence. The this keyword is also not available in static methods. Static fields are stored in the Method Area (part of JVM memory), not on the heap with objects. The most common real-world use of static fields is for class-level counters or constants (combined with final), and static methods are used for utility/helper functions that do not depend on object state.
hint: Static = belongs to the class. Instance = belongs to each object. One copy vs many copies.

question: What is an abstract class in Java? What are all the rules that govern abstract classes and why can't you instantiate one?
answer: An abstract class is a class declared with the abstract keyword that acts as a partial blueprint — it can define both complete methods (with implementation) and abstract methods (declared but not implemented, forcing subclasses to provide the implementation). You cannot create an object of an abstract class using new because an abstract class is by definition incomplete — it has at least one method with no body, so if Java allowed you to instantiate it, calling that method would have nothing to execute. Abstract classes are used when you want to define common behavior for a group of related classes but some parts of that behavior must be customized per subclass. The rules are: abstract methods cannot have a body, any class that extends an abstract class must implement all abstract methods or itself be declared abstract, abstract classes CAN have constructors (called via super() from child), abstract classes CAN have static methods, abstract classes CAN have non-abstract methods with full implementations, and a class cannot be both abstract and final because final prevents extension which would make the abstract methods permanently unimplementable.
hint: Abstract class = template with gaps. The gaps (abstract methods) must be filled by every concrete subclass.

question: What is an interface in Java and how does it differ from an abstract class? When should you choose one over the other?
answer: An interface is a pure contract — it defines what methods a class must have without providing any implementation (before Java 8). All methods in an interface are implicitly public and abstract, and all fields are implicitly public static final. A class implements an interface (not extends), and it must provide implementations for all interface methods. The fundamental difference from abstract classes is that Java supports implementing multiple interfaces but only single inheritance for classes. This solves the diamond problem while still allowing a class to fulfill multiple contracts. Choose an interface when you want pure abstraction with no shared state or shared logic — all the implementing classes are completely independent and just need to fulfill the same contract. Choose an abstract class when the related classes share some common implementation or common state (fields) that should not be duplicated across all subclasses. The real-world interview answer is: interfaces define what a class IS (its capabilities/contracts), while abstract classes define what a class IS A KIND OF (its base type with shared behavior). In modern Java (8+), interfaces can have default methods with implementations, which blurs the line slightly but the design intent remains the same.
hint: Interface = what you CAN DO. Abstract class = what you ARE with some shared behavior already written.

question: In Java interfaces, all methods are implicitly public. What happens if you try to implement an interface method with a weaker access modifier, and why does this rule exist?
answer: If you try to implement an interface method without the public keyword (using default package-private visibility, protected, or private), you get a compile-time error: 'attempting to assign weaker access privileges; was public'. This happens because all interface methods are implicitly public abstract even if you do not write the keyword explicitly. When a class implements an interface, Java does not allow it to reduce the visibility of those methods. The reason this rule exists is contract enforcement. An interface is a public contract — it promises that anyone with a reference of the interface type can call those methods. If an implementing class could reduce visibility to package-private, then code outside the package holding a reference of the interface type would call a method the contract promised was public, but the actual implementing object would refuse access — this breaks the entire point of interface-based programming and polymorphism. Think of it this way: the interface made a promise to the outside world, and the implementing class cannot take that promise back.
hint: Interface made a public promise. The implementing class cannot break that promise by hiding the method.

question: What is the difference between Association, Aggregation, and Composition in Java? How do you identify which relationship exists between two classes?
answer: These three terms describe the 'has-a' relationship between classes with increasing degrees of dependency. Association is the general term for any has-a relationship — one class uses or knows about another. Aggregation is a weak has-a relationship where both objects can exist independently — if one is destroyed, the other survives. A Library has Books, but if the Library closes down the Books still exist. In code, the outer class holds a reference to the inner object but did not create it and is not responsible for its lifecycle. Composition is a strong has-a relationship where the inner object cannot exist without the outer one — the outer class creates, manages, and owns the inner objects. If a House is destroyed its Rooms are destroyed too because a Room outside a House makes no sense. In code, the outer class creates the inner objects inside its constructor, taking full responsibility for their lifecycle. The way to identify which relationship you have: ask 'if I delete the outer object, does the inner object still make sense on its own?' If yes, it is aggregation. If no, it is composition.
hint: Aggregation = 'I borrow it'. Composition = 'I own it and it dies with me'.

question: Explain the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP). What exactly counts as a 'responsibility' and how do you identify when a class is violating SRP?
answer: The Single Responsibility Principle states that a class should have only one reason to change. The word 'responsibility' here means a distinct concern or actor that might require the class to be modified. The practical test is not counting methods but counting how many different types of changes could force you to modify this class. If you have an Invoice class that calculates totals, prints invoices, and saves to a database, then three completely different reasons could force you to change it: the business changes the discount formula (calculation logic), the UI team changes the print format (printing logic), or the DBA changes the database schema (persistence logic). Each of these reasons represents a different stakeholder and a different axis of change. SRP says each class should answer to exactly one stakeholder. The fix is to split the class: keep Invoice for calculation only, create InvoicePrinter for printing, and create InvoiceDao for database operations. The benefit is that changing the print format only requires touching InvoicePrinter — the calculation and persistence code are completely isolated and untouched, reducing the risk of introducing bugs in unrelated functionality.
hint: Ask: how many different types of people (business analyst, UI designer, DBA) would ask me to change this class? That number should be 1.

question: Explain the Open/Closed Principle. What does 'open for extension, closed for modification' mean in practice, and how do interfaces help achieve it?
answer: The Open/Closed Principle states that a class should be open for extension (you can add new behavior) but closed for modification (you should not have to change the existing code to add that behavior). The problem it solves is this: every time you modify existing working code, you risk breaking something that was already working and tested. If adding new functionality requires you to open an existing class and add if-else branches, you are accumulating risk with every new feature. The solution is to design classes around abstractions (interfaces or abstract classes) so that new behavior is added by creating new classes that implement the abstraction, not by modifying the existing class. The TaxCalculator example illustrates this perfectly — if TaxCalculator is a concrete class with a hardcoded India tax rate, adding US tax requires modifying the existing class. But if TaxCalculator is an interface with an amountAfterTax() method, you can add USTax by creating a new class that implements the interface — the original IndiaTax class is never touched, never risked.
hint: OCP = add new behavior by writing new code, not by changing old code.

question: Explain the Liskov Substitution Principle. What does it mean to substitute a subclass for its parent, and what does this look like in Java with runtime polymorphism?
answer: The Liskov Substitution Principle states that if class B is a subtype of class A, then anywhere you use an object of type A, you should be able to replace it with an object of type B without breaking the program's correctness. In practical Java terms, this means a subclass must honor the contract of its parent — it should not remove behavior, throw unexpected exceptions, or produce wrong results when used via the parent reference. The Notification example demonstrates this well: if you have a Notification reference and call sendNotification(), it should work correctly regardless of whether the actual object is a TextNotification or WhatsAppNotification. This works because of runtime polymorphism — the JVM calls the overridden version in the actual object type at runtime, but the calling code only sees and trusts the parent contract. LSP is violated when a subclass overrides a method in a way that changes its fundamental contract — for example, a Square class extending Rectangle that breaks the setWidth() contract because changing width of a square must also change height.
hint: LSP = can I blindly swap parent with child in any code and have it still work correctly? If yes, LSP holds.

question: Explain the Interface Segregation Principle. What problem does a large 'fat' interface create, and how do you fix it?
answer: The Interface Segregation Principle states that a class should not be forced to implement methods it does not use. The problem arises when you create a large interface that tries to cover all possible behaviors for all possible implementing classes — this is called a fat or bloated interface. If the Uber interface has bookRide(), payRide(), acceptRide(), drive(), and endRide(), then any class implementing Uber must implement all five methods. A Rider class has no business implementing acceptRide() or drive() — those are driver behaviors. A Driver class has no business implementing bookRide() or payRide() — those are rider behaviors. Forcing classes to implement irrelevant methods leads to empty stub implementations, confusion, and tight coupling. The fix is to split the fat interface into multiple smaller, role-specific interfaces. RiderInterface contains bookRide() and payRide(). DriverInterface contains acceptRide(), drive(), and endRide(). Now Rider implements only RiderInterface and Driver implements only DriverInterface — each class only depends on what it actually uses. This also makes the system more flexible for future changes.
hint: If a class has to implement a method that throws UnsupportedOperationException or is empty, ISP is probably being violated.

question: Explain the Dependency Inversion Principle. What is the difference between depending on a concrete class vs depending on an abstraction, and why does DIP make code more testable?
answer: The Dependency Inversion Principle has two parts: high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules (both should depend on abstractions), and abstractions should not depend on details (details should depend on abstractions). In plain terms, your important business logic classes should not be tightly coupled to specific implementations of the things they use. The Netflix example makes this clear: if Netflix class directly creates TrendingRecommendation and GenreRecommendation objects inside itself, it is tightly coupled to those specific implementations. Adding a new recommendation type requires opening and modifying the Netflix class. With DIP, Netflix depends only on a RecommendationStrategy interface — it does not know or care which specific implementation it receives. The concrete strategy is injected from outside (dependency injection). This makes code dramatically more testable because in tests you can inject a mock or fake implementation instead of the real one — your test for Netflix's recommendation flow does not need to run real trending algorithms. It also makes the system flexible — swapping implementations requires changing only the injection point, not the business logic.
hint: DIP = high level code should depend on what something does (interface), not what something is (concrete class).

question: In Java, when you write 'Notification notif = new TextNotification()', what exactly determines which method runs when you call notif.sendNotification()? Explain compile-time vs runtime resolution.
answer: This scenario demonstrates runtime polymorphism and involves two separate decisions made at different times. At compile time, the Java compiler looks at the reference type — which is Notification on the left side. The compiler only checks whether the method sendNotification() exists in Notification. It does not care what actual object will be there at runtime. If sendNotification() exists in Notification, the code compiles successfully. At runtime, the JVM looks at the actual object stored in the notif variable — which is a TextNotification created by new TextNotification(). The JVM checks if TextNotification has overridden sendNotification(). If it has, the overridden version in TextNotification runs. If it has not, the JVM walks up the inheritance chain and finds the version in Notification. This is called dynamic dispatch or late binding. The rule to remember is: the left side (reference type) decides what methods you are ALLOWED to call at compile time. The right side (object type) decides which VERSION of that method actually runs at runtime. This is why you cannot call a method that exists only in TextNotification through a Notification reference — the compiler does not know and does not trust what the actual object will be.
hint: Left side = compiler's view (what methods exist). Right side = JVM's view at runtime (which version runs).

question: What is the difference between method overloading (compile-time polymorphism) and method overriding (runtime polymorphism)? Why is one resolved at compile time and the other at runtime?
answer: Method overloading is when multiple methods in the same class have the same name but different parameter lists (different number, type, or order of parameters). The compiler resolves which overloaded method to call at compile time because all the information needed — the method name and argument types — is known at compile time from the call site. This is called compile-time polymorphism or static dispatch. Method overriding is when a subclass provides a new implementation for a method that already exists in its parent class with the exact same signature (name, parameters, return type). The JVM resolves which version to call at runtime because the actual object type is only known at runtime — a Notification reference could hold a TextNotification or WhatsAppNotification and you only know which one when the program is actually running. This is why it is called runtime polymorphism or dynamic dispatch. The four conditions for valid overriding are: same method name, same parameter types in same order, return type must be same or covariant (subtype), and access modifier cannot be reduced.
hint: Overloading = same name different signature, compiler decides. Overriding = same signature in child, JVM decides at runtime.

question: What is inheritance in Java? Explain all five types and why Java only supports single inheritance for classes but multiple inheritance through interfaces.
answer: Inheritance is a mechanism where one class acquires the properties and behaviors of another class, enabling code reuse and establishing an IS-A relationship. There are five types conceptually. Single inheritance is when one class extends exactly one parent class — Java fully supports this. Multilevel inheritance is a chain: A extends B which extends C — Java supports this. Hierarchical inheritance is multiple classes extending the same parent — Java supports this. Multiple inheritance is one class extending two or more parent classes directly — Java does NOT support this for classes. The reason is the diamond problem: if class C extends both A and B, and both A and B have a method display(), when C calls display() the JVM cannot determine which version to use — it is ambiguous. Java solves this by completely prohibiting multiple class inheritance and instead allowing a class to implement multiple interfaces. Since interfaces (pre-Java 8) have no method implementations, there is no ambiguity about which version to use. Hybrid inheritance is a combination of single and multiple — also not applicable directly in Java for classes.
hint: Java chose simplicity over power: no multiple class inheritance to avoid diamond problem, but multiple interfaces are fine.

question: What is the 'super' keyword in Java? Explain all three uses and what happens to the constructor chain when you create an object of a deeply inherited class.
answer: The super keyword is a reference to the parent class of the current class. It has three uses. First, super.method() calls the parent class's version of a method that has been overridden in the current class — useful when you want to extend the parent behavior rather than replace it entirely. Second, super.field accesses a parent class field that is hidden by a field with the same name in the child class. Third and most important, super() calls the parent class constructor and must be the first statement in any child class constructor. When you create an object of a child class, Java requires that the parent class be fully initialized before the child class constructor runs — this ensures the parent's state is ready for the child to build upon. If you do not explicitly call super(), Java automatically inserts a call to the no-argument parent constructor. If the parent class does not have a no-argument constructor, you must explicitly call the appropriate parameterized super() constructor or you get a compile error. In a multilevel chain like C extends B extends A, creating a C object calls A's constructor first, then B's, then C's — top to bottom.
hint: Constructor chain always runs top-down: grandparent first, parent second, child last.

question: What is the final keyword in Java when applied to a class, method, and variable? Why can't an abstract class be final?
answer: The final keyword in Java means 'this cannot be changed or extended'. When applied to a variable, final means the variable can only be assigned once — for primitives this means the value cannot change, for references this means the reference cannot point to a different object (though the object itself can still be mutated). When applied to a method, final means the method cannot be overridden by any subclass — useful when you want to guarantee that a specific implementation is always used regardless of the subclass. When applied to a class, final means the class cannot be extended at all — no subclass can be created from it. The Java String class is famously final for security and performance reasons. An abstract class and final are fundamentally contradictory and cannot be combined. An abstract class exists for the sole purpose of being extended — it requires subclasses to implement its abstract methods. A final class exists to prevent any extension. These two goals are mutually exclusive: you cannot have a class that demands to be extended and simultaneously forbids extension. The compiler will reject any class declared as both abstract and final.
hint: final = 'this is the end of the line'. Abstract = 'you must continue from here'. They cannot coexist.

question: What is the Object class in Java and why do equals() and == behave differently for objects vs primitives?
answer: The Object class is the root of the entire Java class hierarchy — every class in Java implicitly extends Object if it does not extend any other class. This means every Java object inherits methods like toString(), equals(), hashCode(), getClass(), and wait/notify from Object. The == operator in Java compares memory addresses (references) for objects and actual values for primitives. So for primitives, 5 == 5 is true because the values are equal. For objects, new String('hello') == new String('hello') is false because they are two different objects at different memory locations even though their content is identical. The equals() method was designed to be overridden to provide meaningful content-based comparison. By default (in Object), equals() also compares references just like ==. But classes like String, Integer, and most standard library classes override equals() to compare actual content. This is why 'hello'.equals('hello') returns true — String overrides equals() to compare character by character. If you create a custom class without overriding equals(), two objects with identical field values will return false for equals() because it falls back to Object's reference comparison.
hint: == always compares addresses for objects. equals() compares content ONLY if the class overrides it.

question: What are wrapper classes in Java? What is autoboxing and unboxing, and what NullPointerException trap do they introduce?
answer: Wrapper classes are object representations of Java's eight primitive types: Integer for int, Double for double, Boolean for boolean, Character for char, Long for long, Float for float, Short for short, and Byte for byte. They are needed because Java collections like ArrayList cannot hold primitives — they require objects. Wrapper classes also provide useful utility methods like Integer.parseInt(), Integer.MAX_VALUE, and Double.isNaN(). Autoboxing is the automatic conversion Java performs when you assign a primitive to a wrapper type — Java silently wraps the primitive in its wrapper object. Unboxing is the reverse — Java automatically extracts the primitive value from the wrapper object when needed in a primitive context. The dangerous trap is that wrapper objects can be null, but primitives cannot. If you have an Integer variable that is null and Java tries to unbox it (extract the int value), you get a NullPointerException at runtime — and this can be subtle and hard to debug because the code looks like it is just doing arithmetic with numbers.
hint: Autoboxing/unboxing is convenient but null wrapper objects will explode when unboxed. Always check for null.

question: Why is String immutable in Java? What is the String pool and why does immutability make String safe to share across threads?
answer: String is immutable in Java meaning once a String object is created, its character array content can never be changed. Any operation that appears to modify a String (like concatenation or toUpperCase) actually creates and returns a brand new String object. The reasons for this design are security, performance, and thread safety. For security, String is used for sensitive data like database URLs, passwords, and class names for loading. If String were mutable, code that received a validated URL String could have its content changed by another thread before it was used. For performance, Java maintains a String pool (also called String intern pool) in the heap's method area where string literals are stored. When you write String s = 'hello', Java first checks if 'hello' already exists in the pool. If it does, it returns the existing reference rather than creating a new object. This saves memory when the same string value appears many times. For thread safety, because String objects can never be modified, multiple threads can safely read the same String object simultaneously with no synchronization needed. If you need to build strings dynamically with many modifications, use StringBuilder (not thread-safe, faster) or StringBuffer (thread-safe, slower due to synchronization).
hint: String is immutable so the pool can safely share references. Mutable strings would make sharing dangerous.

question: What is the difference between a Program, a Process, and a Thread in Java? Explain with a real-world analogy and why threads share memory but processes do not.
answer: A Program is simply an executable file sitting on disk — it is static, just bytes of code that have not started running yet. Think of it like a recipe written in a book. A Process is a running instance of that program — when you double-click Chrome, the OS loads the program into memory and creates a process. The process gets its own isolated memory space, its own program counter, its own file handles, and its own system resources. If you open Chrome twice, you have two completely independent processes that cannot directly read each other's memory. A Thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process — it is a lightweight sub-task. Within a single Chrome process, there are many threads: one loading the UI, one making network calls, one rendering the page. All threads within the same process share the same heap memory, which means they can communicate easily but also means they can corrupt each other's data if not coordinated carefully. The key insight is: processes are isolated for safety, threads are shared for efficiency. Creating a new process is expensive (new memory space, OS overhead). Creating a new thread is cheap because it reuses the existing process memory.
hint: Program = recipe on paper. Process = cooking happening in a kitchen. Thread = individual cook inside that kitchen.

question: What is a CPU core and what is context switching? How does the OS execute multiple threads when there are fewer cores than threads?
answer: A CPU core is an individual processing unit inside the CPU chip that can execute one thread at a time. A quad-core CPU has four cores meaning it can genuinely execute four threads simultaneously in true parallelism. Context switching is the mechanism the OS uses to give the illusion of running more threads than there are cores. When a thread's time slice expires or it blocks on I/O, the OS saves the thread's entire execution state — its program counter, register values, stack pointer — into a data structure called the Thread Control Block. It then loads the saved state of another thread and resumes it. The cost of context switching is real: saving and restoring state takes time, and switching between threads causes cache misses because the new thread's data is likely not in the CPU cache. This is why creating thousands of threads is not always faster — beyond a certain point the overhead of context switching overwhelms the benefit of parallelism. The OS thread scheduler decides which thread runs next based on priority, fairness algorithms, and whether threads are blocked or ready. In Java, the JVM relies on the underlying OS scheduler and does not guarantee which thread runs next or for how long.
hint: Context switching = OS pauses one thread, saves its state, resumes another. Fast but not free — it has overhead.

question: What is the difference between Concurrency and Parallelism? Why can a single-core machine achieve concurrency but not parallelism?
answer: Concurrency means multiple tasks are making progress over time but not necessarily at the exact same instant. Tasks are in progress simultaneously in the sense that they are all started and none has finished, but at any given microsecond only one is actually executing on the CPU. The OS interleaves them rapidly to give the appearance of simultaneous execution. A single-core machine can achieve concurrency through context switching — it rapidly switches between tasks giving the illusion that they are running together. Parallelism means multiple tasks are literally executing at the exact same instant on multiple CPU cores. True parallelism requires multiple cores. If you have 4 tasks and 4 cores, all 4 can run simultaneously — that is parallelism. The practical implication is important: if you have CPU-bound tasks (pure computation), adding more threads beyond your core count does not help and actually hurts due to context switching overhead. But for I/O-bound tasks (waiting for network, disk, database), concurrency helps enormously because threads can make progress on other tasks while one thread is blocked waiting for I/O.
hint: Concurrency = juggling (one ball in air at a time but all in progress). Parallelism = multiple jugglers each with their own ball.

question: Compare Processes and Threads across six dimensions: memory, isolation, communication, overhead, failure impact, and creation cost. Why would you choose one over the other?
answer: Processes and threads differ fundamentally in how they relate to system resources. For memory, each process has its own completely separate memory space — heap, stack, code, and data segments are all private. Threads within a process share the heap and method area but each thread has its own stack. For isolation, processes are fully isolated — a bug in one process cannot corrupt another process's memory. Threads are not isolated — a thread that corrupts shared heap data corrupts the data for all threads in the process. For communication, process communication (IPC) requires explicit mechanisms like sockets, pipes, message queues, or shared memory files — it is complex. Thread communication is simple since they share the same memory, but requires synchronization to be safe. For overhead, creating a process is expensive — the OS must allocate new memory space and duplicate resources. Creating a thread is cheap — it reuses the process's existing memory. For failure impact, if a process crashes the OS cleans it up and other processes are unaffected. If a thread crashes with an unhandled exception, it can take down the entire process including all other threads. For creation cost, spawning a new process takes milliseconds. Creating a new thread takes microseconds.
hint: Processes = safe but expensive and hard to communicate. Threads = fast and easy to share but risky if not synchronized.

subcategory: User Level vs Kernel Level Threads
question: What is the difference between User-Level Threads (ULT) and Kernel-Level Threads (KLT)? What is a Lightweight Process (LWP) and how does the JVM use this model?
answer: User-Level Threads are threads managed entirely by the application or runtime (like the JVM) without the OS kernel's knowledge. The kernel sees only one entity — the process — and schedules it as a single unit. The JVM's thread library handles scheduling among ULTs internally. The advantage is speed — creating and switching between ULTs has no kernel calls so it is extremely fast. The disadvantage is that if one ULT blocks on a system call (like reading from disk), the entire process blocks because the kernel only sees one schedulable entity. Kernel-Level Threads are threads that the OS kernel knows about and schedules directly. Each KLT can be scheduled independently, so if one blocks on I/O, others continue running. The downside is that creating and switching KLTs requires OS system calls which are slower. A Lightweight Process is a bridge between ULTs and KLTs — it is a schedulable entity that the kernel manages, and user-level threads are mapped onto LWPs. Java uses the Many-to-Many or One-to-One model depending on the JVM implementation. Modern Java (HotSpot JVM on Linux) maps each Java thread to a native OS thread (KLT) in a one-to-one relationship, which means Java threads get true OS-level scheduling and blocking one Java thread does not block others.
hint: ULT = runtime manages, fast but one block kills all. KLT = OS manages, slower but independent scheduling.

question: What are the two ways to create a thread in Java — extending Thread vs implementing Runnable? What are the differences and which should you prefer?
answer: The first way to create a thread is by extending the Thread class and overriding its run() method. You then create an instance of your class and call start() on it. The second way is by implementing the Runnable interface, which has a single method run(), and passing an instance of your Runnable to the Thread constructor. You should almost always prefer implementing Runnable over extending Thread for several reasons. First, Java does not support multiple inheritance for classes, so if you extend Thread your class cannot extend any other class. By implementing Runnable, your class can still extend another class if needed. Second, implementing Runnable better follows the single responsibility principle — your class defines the task to execute (what to run), while the Thread class handles the execution mechanics (how to run it). These are two different concerns. Third, Runnable instances can be passed to thread pools via ExecutorService, which is the modern preferred way to manage threads — you cannot directly pass a Thread subclass instance to ExecutorService. The critical mistake beginners make is calling run() instead of start() — calling run() directly executes the method on the current thread with no new thread created at all.
hint: Prefer Runnable because it separates WHAT to run from HOW to run it, and it works with thread pools.

question: What are the different states a Java thread can be in during its lifecycle? Explain what triggers each transition between states.
answer: A Java thread goes through six distinct states defined in the Thread.State enum. NEW is the state when a Thread object has been created but start() has not been called yet — the thread exists as an object but no OS thread has been allocated. RUNNABLE is the state after start() is called — the thread is eligible to run and may be currently executing or waiting for the CPU scheduler to give it a time slice. BLOCKED is when a thread is waiting to acquire a synchronized lock that another thread currently holds — it is stuck at the entrance of a synchronized block. WAITING is when a thread is indefinitely waiting for another thread to perform a specific action — this happens when you call wait(), join(), or LockSupport.park() without a timeout. TIMED_WAITING is similar to WAITING but with a specified timeout — this happens when you call sleep(millis), wait(millis), or join(millis). TERMINATED is the final state when the run() method has completed normally or threw an uncaught exception — the thread is dead and cannot be restarted. Understanding these states is crucial for debugging multithreaded applications because thread dumps show these states for every thread, allowing you to diagnose deadlocks (threads stuck in BLOCKED), resource contention, and performance issues.
hint: NEW→RUNNABLE on start(), RUNNABLE→BLOCKED on lock contention, RUNNABLE→WAITING on wait()/join(), TERMINATED when run() ends.

question: What is the difference between Runnable and Callable in Java? How do Future and FutureTask bridge Callable to the Thread API?
answer: Runnable's run() method has a void return type and cannot throw checked exceptions — it is fire-and-forget. This makes it unsuitable for tasks where you need a result back or need to handle checked exceptions in the task body. Callable is a functional interface introduced to solve this — its call() method returns a generic type T and is declared to throw Exception, meaning the task can both produce a result and propagate checked exceptions to the caller. However, there is a problem: the Thread constructor only accepts Runnable, not Callable. FutureTask bridges this gap — it implements both Runnable (so it can be passed to Thread) and Future (so it can hold and return the result). You wrap your Callable inside a FutureTask, pass the FutureTask to a Thread, and then call future.get() to retrieve the result when you need it. The get() method blocks the calling thread until the result is available. When using ExecutorService, you can submit a Callable directly using executor.submit(callable) which returns a Future — ExecutorService handles the FutureTask wrapping internally.
hint: Runnable = fire and forget. Callable = task with a return value. FutureTask wraps Callable to make it work with Thread.

question: What is an ExecutorService and why should you use it instead of creating threads manually? Explain the three main thread pool types and when to use each.
answer: ExecutorService is Java's high-level thread management API that manages a pool of worker threads, allowing you to submit tasks without creating and managing individual threads yourself. Creating a new Thread for every task is wasteful — thread creation has overhead (stack memory allocation, OS thread creation) and if you create thousands of threads for thousands of tasks your system runs out of memory and spends more time context switching than doing actual work. A thread pool creates a fixed set of threads upfront and reuses them across tasks, dramatically reducing overhead. The three main pool types are: newFixedThreadPool(n) creates exactly n threads that live permanently — good for controlled workloads like web servers where you want to limit concurrency and have bounded queue behavior. newCachedThreadPool() creates threads on demand and reuses idle ones — good for short-lived high-throughput tasks like API calls, but dangerous in production without limits because under high load it can create thousands of threads and crash the JVM with OutOfMemoryError. newScheduledThreadPool(n) creates n threads that can run tasks after a delay or periodically — good for cron jobs, reminders, health checks, and scheduled background tasks. The execute() method submits a Runnable with no return value. The submit() method submits a Callable or Runnable and returns a Future you can use to get the result or check completion.
hint: Fixed = bounded control. Cached = speed but risky. Scheduled = timing. Always call shutdown() when done.

question: Why can newCachedThreadPool() crash your JVM under high load? What happens internally when you submit tasks faster than threads can process them?
answer: newCachedThreadPool() uses a SynchronousQueue internally which has zero capacity — it does not actually queue tasks. Instead, every submitted task immediately creates a new thread if no idle thread is available. Under normal load this is fine because threads complete quickly and are reused. But under high load, if tasks are submitted faster than threads complete them, the pool keeps creating new threads indefinitely. Since there is no upper bound on thread count, under extreme load you can have hundreds of thousands of threads all consuming stack memory (each thread needs ~512KB to 1MB of stack by default). This leads to OutOfMemoryError and JVM crash. The fix is to use ThreadPoolExecutor directly with explicit parameters: core pool size, maximum pool size, keep-alive time, and a bounded BlockingQueue. With a bounded queue, when the queue is full and all max threads are busy, new task submissions are rejected according to a RejectionPolicy. The lesson is: newCachedThreadPool() is designed for short-lived tasks in a controlled environment, not for production systems handling arbitrary load. In production, always use bounded thread pools with explicit limits and rejection handling.
hint: CachedThreadPool = no thread limit + no queue = unlimited threads under load = JVM crash. Always bound in production.

question: Why is the output order of tasks submitted to a thread pool not always 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 even though you submitted them in that order? What three factors determine completion order?
answer: When you submit tasks to a thread pool, the submission order to the queue is preserved but the completion order is not guaranteed to match. Three factors determine when each task completes. First, JVM scheduling — the JVM relies on the underlying OS scheduler to decide which runnable thread gets CPU time next, and this decision is non-deterministic. Even if task 1 starts before task 2, the OS might give more CPU time to task 2's thread, allowing it to finish first. Second, thread wake-up timing — when a thread becomes available in the pool, the OS decides when to wake it up and give it the CPU. This wake-up timing varies unpredictably. Third, CPU allocation and core affinity — on multi-core systems, different tasks run on different cores and there is no guarantee about the relative speeds of different cores or their cache states at that moment. This non-determinism is a fundamental property of concurrent systems and is why you must never write code that assumes tasks complete in submission order. If you need ordered results, collect all Futures and call get() on them in order, which will give you results in submission order even if tasks completed out of order.
hint: Submission order = queue order. Completion order = determined by OS scheduler, CPU allocation, task duration. Never assume they match.

question: What is thread starvation? How is it different from deadlock, and what are the main causes and prevention strategies?
answer: Thread starvation is a condition where a thread is perpetually denied access to resources it needs to make progress, even though those resources are not permanently locked by anyone. Unlike deadlock where no threads can make progress because they are all waiting for each other in a circular dependency, starvation allows some threads to make progress while others are perpetually sidelined. Common causes include unfair lock policies where some threads always get priority over others, thread priority abuse where high-priority threads monopolize the CPU starving low-priority ones, long-running tasks holding locks or occupying thread pool workers preventing other tasks from getting a turn, and thread pool starvation where all worker threads are blocked waiting for results from tasks that are themselves waiting in the same pool queue creating a self-imposed deadlock. Prevention strategies include using fair locks with ReentrantLock(true) which grants access in FIFO order, avoiding extreme thread priority differences, breaking long tasks into smaller units, using properly sized thread pools, and using bounded queues to prevent any single producer from flooding the system.
hint: Starvation = some threads progress, one starves. Deadlock = nobody progresses. Both are liveness failures.

question: What is the tradeoff between a fair lock and a non-fair lock? When should you choose fairness over performance?
answer: A fair lock (ReentrantLock(true)) grants lock access in the exact order threads requested it — first come first served. This guarantees no thread waits indefinitely and completely eliminates starvation. A non-fair lock (ReentrantLock(false), which is also the default) allows any waiting thread to acquire the lock when it becomes available, with no regard for how long threads have been waiting. Non-fair locks are significantly faster in practice because they allow a newly woken thread to acquire the lock immediately without the overhead of checking and maintaining a wait queue. This phenomenon is called barging — a thread that just released work and immediately requests the lock again might get it before a thread that has been waiting for a long time. The tradeoff is clear: fair locks give you starvation freedom and predictable waiting times at the cost of lower throughput. Non-fair locks give you higher throughput and lower average latency at the cost of potential starvation for some threads. In practice, use fair locks when you have strict latency requirements for all threads or when starvation is a real observed problem. Use non-fair locks (the default) for high-throughput scenarios where occasional waits are acceptable and starvation is unlikely given the task characteristics.
hint: Fair = predictable order, lower speed. Non-fair = faster, but some threads might wait much longer than others.

question: What is a race condition in Java? Walk through a concrete example showing exactly how two threads can corrupt shared data even with simple operations like incrementing a counter.
answer: A race condition occurs when the correctness of a program depends on the relative timing or interleaving of multiple threads, and the actual execution order produces incorrect results. Even something as simple as counter++ is not atomic — it is actually three separate low-level operations: read the current value of counter, add 1 to it, write the new value back. If two threads both read the same value before either writes back, both compute the same new value and one increment is effectively lost. For example: counter is 5. Thread 1 reads 5. OS context switches to Thread 2. Thread 2 reads 5. Thread 2 writes 6. OS switches back to Thread 1. Thread 1 writes 6 (based on the 5 it read earlier, not the 6 Thread 2 wrote). Counter is now 6 instead of the expected 7 — one increment was lost. This is called a lost update. Race conditions are notoriously hard to reproduce because they depend on exact thread scheduling, which varies between runs, hardware, and load levels. They often pass all tests but fail in production under high load when thread interleaving becomes more frequent.
hint: counter++ is 3 steps. If two threads interleave between those steps, one update gets lost. That is a race condition.

question: What does thread safety mean in Java? What are the four main approaches to achieving thread safety and when do you use each?
answer: Thread safety means that a piece of code, object, or method behaves correctly and predictably when accessed by multiple threads simultaneously, without corrupting data or producing incorrect results. An object is thread-safe if its invariants hold (its internal state is always valid) regardless of how many threads access it concurrently. There are four main approaches to achieving thread safety. First, synchronization using the synchronized keyword or locks — you serialize access to shared data so only one thread can modify it at a time. This is the most common approach but introduces contention and can reduce throughput. Second, using atomic classes from java.util.concurrent.atomic like AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, AtomicReference — these use hardware-level compare-and-swap (CAS) operations that are atomic without requiring locks, giving better performance for simple operations. Third, using concurrent collections from java.util.concurrent like ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList — these are designed for concurrent access with internal synchronization tuned for performance. Fourth, immutability — if an object's state never changes after construction, it is inherently thread-safe and requires no synchronization at all. The best approach depends on the access pattern: high contention favors lock-free atomic operations, complex multi-step operations require locks, and pure reads favor immutability or concurrent collections.
hint: Thread safety = state is always valid regardless of thread interleaving. Approaches: synchronization, atomics, concurrent collections, immutability.

question: How does the synchronized keyword work in Java? What is the difference between a synchronized method and a synchronized block, and what is the monitor object?
answer: The synchronized keyword in Java uses an intrinsic lock (also called a monitor) associated with every Java object. When a thread enters a synchronized block or method, it acquires the monitor lock of the specified object. While it holds this lock, no other thread can enter any synchronized block that uses the same monitor object — those threads block and wait in the BLOCKED state until the lock is released. When the thread exits the synchronized block (normally or via exception), the lock is released automatically. For a synchronized instance method, the monitor object is this — the current instance. For a synchronized static method, the monitor is the Class object (MyClass.class). A synchronized block lets you specify explicitly which object to use as the monitor, and can be a smaller critical section than the entire method. Synchronized blocks are preferred over synchronized methods for performance because they minimize the scope of synchronization — only the code that actually needs mutual exclusion is locked, not the entire method body. The limitations of synchronized are: no timeout (a thread will wait forever for a lock), no ability to interrupt a waiting thread, and no fairness guarantee (any waiting thread might get the lock next).
hint: synchronized acquires monitor lock. Method = locks 'this'. Static = locks Class object. Block = you choose the monitor.

question: What is ReentrantLock and what advantages does it have over synchronized? Explain reentrancy, tryLock(), tryLock(timeout), and why you must always unlock in a finally block.
answer: ReentrantLock is an explicit locking mechanism from java.util.concurrent.locks that provides all the functionality of synchronized plus additional control. Reentrancy means a thread that already holds the lock can acquire it again without blocking on itself — each lock() call must be matched by an unlock() call. This prevents a thread from deadlocking itself when it calls another synchronized method from within a synchronized context. The key advantages over synchronized are: tryLock() lets you attempt to acquire the lock without blocking — if the lock is unavailable it returns false immediately and the thread can do something else or retry later. tryLock(time, unit) waits up to the specified time for the lock and returns false if not acquired within that time, preventing indefinite blocking. ReentrantLock supports a fairness policy (FIFO ordering of waiting threads) that synchronized does not provide. You can also interrupt a thread that is waiting for a ReentrantLock using lockInterruptibly(). The critical rule is that you MUST always call unlock() in a finally block — if you forget and an exception is thrown, the lock will never be released and every subsequent thread that tries to acquire it will block forever, causing the application to hang.
hint: ReentrantLock = synchronized + tryLock + timeout + fairness + interruptible. Always unlock() in finally — no exceptions.

question: What is ReadWriteLock in Java? How does it improve performance over ReentrantLock when you have many readers and few writers?
answer: ReadWriteLock maintains two locks internally: a read lock and a write lock. The read lock allows multiple threads to hold it simultaneously — all readers can read concurrently with no blocking between them. The write lock is exclusive — only one thread can hold it at a time, and while it is held no other thread can hold either the read lock or write lock. This design dramatically improves performance in read-heavy workloads. Consider a cache or configuration store where reads happen thousands of times per second but writes happen rarely. With a plain ReentrantLock, every read would block all other reads — completely unnecessary since concurrent reads are safe. With ReadWriteLock, all those concurrent reads proceed without blocking each other, and only when a write occurs do all reads need to wait. The tradeoff to be aware of is write starvation: if there is a constant stream of readers, new readers can always acquire the read lock while it is already held, potentially preventing a waiting writer from ever getting the write lock. ReentrantReadWriteLock has an optional fair mode that prevents this by queuing threads in order.
hint: ReadWriteLock = many readers OK simultaneously, writers must be alone. Perfect for read-heavy workloads like caches.

question: What is a Semaphore in Java? How is it different from a lock, and what real-world problem does it solve?
answer: A Semaphore is a concurrency control mechanism that limits the number of threads that can access a shared resource simultaneously. Unlike a lock which allows exactly one thread at a time, a Semaphore has a configurable number of permits — it allows up to N threads to access the resource concurrently. Think of it as a token bucket at a club entrance: the club has exactly N tokens, each visitor picks one up at the door and returns it when leaving. If all N tokens are taken, new visitors must wait outside until someone returns a token. In Java, Semaphore.acquire() takes a permit (blocking if none available) and Semaphore.release() returns a permit. Semaphores are used for resource pool management like limiting concurrent database connections, controlling access to rate-limited external APIs, or implementing bounded thread pool behavior. Unlike locks, a semaphore can be acquired by one thread and released by a different thread — there is no ownership concept. This makes semaphores unsuitable as mutual exclusion mechanisms (where ownership matters) but perfect for signaling and rate limiting.
hint: Semaphore = N tickets to a venue. When all N are taken, newcomers wait. Any thread can return a ticket.

question: What is a deadlock in Java? What are the four necessary conditions for deadlock to occur, and how does the bank transfer example demonstrate all four?
answer: A deadlock is a situation where two or more threads are permanently blocked, each waiting for a resource held by another thread in the group, resulting in zero progress for all involved threads. Four conditions must ALL be present simultaneously for a deadlock to occur. Mutual exclusion means only one thread can hold a resource at a time — satisfied by synchronized locks. Hold and wait means a thread holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire additional resources held by other threads. No preemption means resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread — a thread must voluntarily release them. Circular wait means there is a circular chain of threads, each waiting for a resource held by the next thread in the chain. The classic bank transfer deadlock demonstrates all four: Thread 1 locks Account A and waits for Account B. Thread 2 locks Account B and waits for Account A. Both hold one lock and wait for the other — circular wait with no preemption possible. Deadlock prevention strategies include acquiring locks in a consistent global order (eliminates circular wait), using tryLock with timeout (allows breaking out of potential deadlock), or using higher-level abstractions that handle locking internally.
hint: Deadlock needs ALL 4 conditions. Break any one: consistent lock order breaks circular wait; tryLock breaks hold-and-wait.

question: What are wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() in Java? Explain the producer-consumer pattern using these methods and why you MUST use while instead of if for the wait condition.
answer: wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() are methods on every Java object used for inter-thread communication through a shared object's monitor. A thread that calls wait() releases the lock it holds and enters a WAITING state — it is suspended until another thread calls notify() on the same object. notify() wakes up exactly one thread that is waiting on that object's monitor (chosen arbitrarily). notifyAll() wakes up ALL waiting threads. The producer-consumer pattern uses wait/notify to coordinate: the producer waits when the buffer is full, the consumer waits when the buffer is empty, and they notify each other when the condition changes. The critical rule is that wait() must ALWAYS be called inside a while loop, never an if statement. The reason is spurious wakeups — the JVM is permitted by the Java Memory Model to wake up a waiting thread without any explicit notify() being called, purely due to OS or hardware behavior. If you use if and the thread wakes up spuriously, it proceeds without checking if the condition it was waiting for is actually true, leading to incorrect behavior. With while, the thread always rechecks the condition after every wakeup and goes back to waiting if the condition is still false.
hint: wait() releases lock and waits. notify() wakes one waiter. ALWAYS use while not if because of spurious wakeups.

question: What does the volatile keyword do in Java? Explain the visibility problem it solves and what it does NOT guarantee.
answer: The volatile keyword in Java solves the visibility problem in multithreaded programs. Without volatile, each CPU core may cache a variable's value in its L1/L2 cache for performance. If Thread 1 running on Core 1 updates a variable, Core 2 running Thread 2 might still read the stale cached value from its own cache, not seeing the update. This is the cache visibility problem. Marking a variable as volatile tells the JVM that every read of this variable must go directly to main memory, and every write must be immediately flushed to main memory, bypassing CPU caches. This ensures all threads always see the most recently written value. Volatile also prevents the JVM and CPU from reordering instructions across the volatile access point, which matters for the double-checked locking Singleton pattern where the instance field must be volatile to prevent partially constructed objects from being visible to other threads. However, volatile does NOT guarantee atomicity. volatile int counter; counter++ is still three non-atomic steps even with volatile. If two threads both read, increment, and write back, you still get race conditions. Volatile only solves visibility, not atomicity. For atomic compound operations, you still need synchronized or AtomicInteger.
hint: volatile = visibility guarantee (always read/write main memory). Does NOT fix race conditions from compound operations like counter++.

question: What is the difference between a Mutex, a Lock, and a Monitor in Java concurrency? How do they relate to each other?
answer: These three terms are related but exist at different levels of abstraction. A Mutex (mutual exclusion) is the broadest concept — it is any mechanism that ensures only one thread can access a critical section at a time. It is an OS-level or algorithmic concept, not a specific Java class. A Lock in Java is a specific API for mutual exclusion — the Lock interface from java.util.concurrent.locks, implemented by classes like ReentrantLock and ReadWriteLock. It provides explicit, fine-grained control over locking with methods like lock(), unlock(), tryLock(), and lockInterruptibly(). In Java we talk about locks when we mean these explicit API objects. A Monitor is a higher-level synchronization construct built into every Java object. Every Java object has an intrinsic monitor that includes both mutual exclusion (the synchronized keyword acquires the monitor lock) and inter-thread communication (wait(), notify(), notifyAll() operate on the monitor). The monitor combines a mutex with a condition variable in one package. When you use synchronized in Java, you are using the object's built-in monitor. When you use ReentrantLock, you are using an explicit lock API without the built-in monitor — for condition variables with ReentrantLock you use the Condition object obtained from lock.newCondition(). In practice: synchronized = monitor (lock + wait/notify). ReentrantLock = lock only + explicit Condition for signaling.
hint: Monitor = lock + condition variable in one package (synchronized + wait/notify). Lock = explicit API (ReentrantLock). Mutex = the concept.

question: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java? How does the compiler treat each, and when should you use each type?
answer: Checked exceptions are exceptions that the Java compiler knows about at compile time and forces you to handle explicitly — either by wrapping the code in a try-catch block or by declaring the exception in the method signature using throws. Examples include IOException, SQLException, and FileNotFoundException. The compiler will refuse to compile code that might throw a checked exception unless you explicitly acknowledge and handle it. The philosophy behind checked exceptions is that they represent conditions that a well-written application should anticipate and recover from — like a file not being found or a network connection failing. Unchecked exceptions extend RuntimeException and are not checked by the compiler — you can ignore them and the code will still compile. They are discovered only at runtime. Examples include NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, ClassCastException, and IllegalArgumentException. Unchecked exceptions represent programming errors or invalid states that typically indicate a bug in the code — conditions you should prevent rather than catch. The guideline is: use checked exceptions for recoverable conditions where the caller can reasonably be expected to handle the failure, and use unchecked exceptions for programming errors that the caller cannot be expected to recover from. In modern Java practice, many developers prefer unchecked exceptions for most cases because excessive checked exceptions make APIs verbose and force callers to handle errors they cannot do anything about.
hint: Checked = compiler forces you to deal with it (recoverable). Unchecked = your bug, fix the code not catch the exception.

question: Explain the try-catch-finally structure in Java. When does the finally block NOT execute, and what is the purpose of multiple catch blocks?
answer: The try block contains code that might throw an exception. The catch block handles specific exception types that are thrown from the try block. The finally block contains code that executes after the try and catch blocks regardless of whether an exception was thrown or caught — it is used for cleanup operations like closing file handles, database connections, or network sockets. You can have multiple catch blocks to handle different exception types differently — Java checks each catch clause in order from top to bottom and executes the first one that matches the thrown exception type. A critical rule is that more specific exception types must appear before more general ones — if you catch Exception before IOException, the compiler rejects it because IOException would never be reached. The finally block executes in virtually all cases including when an exception is thrown but not caught, when a return statement is executed inside try, and when a caught exception is re-thrown. The three cases where finally does NOT execute are: System.exit() is called which terminates the JVM immediately, the JVM crashes, or the thread running the try block is killed. In modern Java, try-with-resources is preferred over finally for closing AutoCloseable resources because it handles closing automatically and more cleanly.
hint: Finally = guaranteed cleanup. Most specific catch first. Finally runs even with return — unless JVM is killed.

question: What is exception propagation and stack unwinding in Java? Walk through what happens step by step when an exception is thrown in a deeply nested method call.
answer: When an exception is thrown, Java initiates a process called stack unwinding where it searches for a matching catch block by walking backward up the call stack. The JVM creates an Exception object containing the exception type, message, and a stack trace showing the complete chain of method calls that led to the exception. Starting from the method where the exception was thrown, the JVM checks if that method has a try-catch block that matches the exception type. If it does, execution jumps to the catch block and unwinding stops. If it does not, the JVM pops that method's stack frame and moves to the calling method, repeating the search. This continues up the call stack until either a matching catch is found or the exception reaches the top of the stack (the main method or the thread's run() method). If no catch is found anywhere, the JVM invokes the thread's uncaught exception handler which by default prints the stack trace and terminates the thread. The stack trace in the exception output shows you the complete call chain from bottom (where exception occurred) to top (where the thread started) which is why reading stack traces from bottom to top helps you identify the root cause most quickly.
hint: Exception travels UP the call stack looking for a matching catch. Read stack traces BOTTOM to TOP to find the root cause.

question: What is the difference between 'throw' and 'throws' in Java? How do you create and throw a custom exception?
answer: throw and throws serve completely different purposes. throw is a statement you write inside a method body to actually create and throw an exception object at a specific point in execution — it is the action of throwing. throws is a keyword in the method signature declaration that tells the compiler and callers that this method might throw a particular checked exception — it is the declaration of intent. A method uses throws to warn callers that they must handle the exception, and it uses throw inside its body to actually trigger the exception when the bad condition is detected. Custom exceptions are created by extending Exception (for checked) or RuntimeException (for unchecked). You should create custom exceptions when you want to give specific, meaningful names to domain-specific error conditions in your application — for example InsufficientFundsException communicates more clearly than a generic IllegalStateException, and allows callers to catch specifically that type of error without catching everything else.
hint: throw = the action (I am throwing this now). throws = the declaration (this method might throw). Different purposes entirely.

question: What are Fail Fast and Fail Safe strategies for error handling? How does a Timeout strategy complement these in distributed systems?
answer: Fail Fast is a strategy where a system detects errors as early as possible and immediately stops further execution to prevent the error from propagating and causing worse problems downstream. The idea is that it is better to crash loudly and immediately at the point of failure than to continue with invalid state and produce incorrect results silently. In practice, fail fast means validating inputs at the start of a method, throwing exceptions immediately when invalid state is detected rather than trying to work around it, and failing noisily with clear error messages rather than returning null or default values that hide problems.
