Fail Safe is a complementary strategy where the system continues operating in a degraded mode when a failure occurs rather than stopping completely. Instead of throwing an exception, a fail safe system returns a default value, cached data, or a simplified response that keeps the system partially functional. Netflix's circuit breaker pattern is a fail safe approach — when a dependent service fails, return cached data rather than crashing the whole page. The Timeout strategy prevents indefinite blocking — when a service call or operation takes longer than an acceptable maximum time, the system gives up and handles the timeout rather than waiting forever. Timeouts are essential in distributed systems because a slow service without a timeout can exhaust all threads waiting for responses, bringing down the entire system. The combination is: fail fast on invalid inputs, use timeouts to prevent resource exhaustion, and fail safe at the boundaries where user experience matters.
hint: Fail fast = crash early and loudly. Fail safe = degrade gracefully. Timeout = don't wait forever. Use all three strategically.

question: What are the main memory areas in the JVM? Explain what is stored in the Heap, Stack, and Method Area and how they interact during object creation and method calls.
answer: The JVM divides memory into several distinct areas each serving a specific purpose. The Heap is the largest memory area and is shared by all threads. All objects created with the new keyword and all arrays are stored here. The heap is managed by the Garbage Collector which automatically reclaims memory from objects that are no longer referenced. There is exactly one heap per JVM process. The JVM Stack (or thread stack) is private to each thread — every thread gets its own stack. The stack stores stack frames, one per method call. Each frame contains local variables, method parameters, and the operand stack for that method's computation. When a method returns, its frame is removed from the stack. Stack memory is automatically managed through frame push/pop and does not need garbage collection. The Method Area (also called Metaspace in Java 8+) is shared by all threads and stores class-level data: class definitions (bytecode), static variables, constant pools, and method bytecode. It is loaded when a class is first used and stays for the lifetime of the JVM. The Program Counter register is a small per-thread memory that holds the address of the current instruction being executed. Understanding these areas explains many Java behaviors: why static variables are shared across all instances (Method Area), why local variables are thread-safe without synchronization (each thread has its own Stack), and why object modifications are visible across threads (shared Heap).
hint: Heap = shared objects (GC managed). Stack = per-thread frames (local vars). Method Area = class data + statics (shared).

question: How does Java's Garbage Collector work? What makes an object eligible for garbage collection, and what are the two main strategies GC uses?
answer: Java's Garbage Collector automatically identifies and reclaims memory from objects that are no longer reachable by any live thread in the application. An object is eligible for garbage collection when there are no more references pointing to it from any live thread's stack, static fields, or other reachable objects. The GC uses reachability analysis starting from a set of root references (local variables, static variables, active threads) and marks everything reachable. Anything not marked is garbage. The two main GC strategies are mark-and-sweep and generational collection. Mark-and-sweep works in two phases: the mark phase traverses from roots and marks all reachable objects, then the sweep phase reclaims memory from all unmarked objects. The weakness is memory fragmentation — freed memory is scattered in small pieces. Generational collection is the strategy used by modern JVMs and is based on the observation that most objects die young. The heap is divided into generations: the Young generation (Eden space + Survivor spaces) for newly created objects, and the Old generation (Tenured heap) for objects that have survived multiple GC cycles. Minor GC runs frequently on the Young generation which is small and fast to collect. Major GC (Full GC) runs less frequently on the Old generation and is much slower. Objects that survive enough Minor GC cycles are promoted to the Old generation.
hint: GC collects objects with no live references. Young generation = short-lived objects, collected frequently. Old generation = long-lived, collected rarely.

question: What is a REST API? Explain the key constraints and design principles including HTTP methods, status codes, DTOs, and when to use filtering, sorting, and pagination.
answer: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for building web APIs that communicate over HTTP. A REST API exposes resources (nouns like /users, /products) and uses HTTP methods (verbs) to define operations on those resources. GET retrieves resources without side effects. POST creates new resources. PUT updates an existing resource completely (idempotent). PATCH partially updates a resource. DELETE removes a resource (idempotent). REST APIs should be stateless — each request contains all the information needed to process it, no session state is stored on the server between requests. HTTP status codes communicate results: 2xx means success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content), 4xx means client error (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found), 5xx means server error (500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable). DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) decouple the API contract from internal domain models — they define exactly what fields the API exposes and accepts, preventing internal implementation details from leaking to clients. For large datasets, filtering (?category=shoes&brand=nike), sorting (?sort=price,asc), and pagination (?page=2&limit=50) are essential to control the amount of data returned and server load.
hint: REST = resources + HTTP verbs. GET reads, POST creates, PUT/PATCH updates, DELETE removes. Use DTOs to protect internal models.

question: Compare REST, GraphQL, and gRPC. What problem does each solve and when should you choose one over the others?
answer: REST, GraphQL, and gRPC are three different approaches to building APIs and each solves a different core problem. REST is resource-based and uses HTTP — it is simple, widely understood, human-readable JSON, works with every client from browsers to mobile apps, and has excellent tooling and caching support. The weakness is over-fetching (you get all fields even if you need only two) and under-fetching (you need multiple requests to get related data). Choose REST for public APIs, simple CRUD applications, and when broad compatibility matters. GraphQL solves REST's over-fetching and under-fetching problems — the client specifies exactly which fields it wants in a single query, reducing both network payload and round trips. It uses a single endpoint and a schema-based type system. The weakness is that it adds complexity, caching is harder, and it has more overhead for simple operations. Choose GraphQL when clients have very different data needs (mobile vs web), when reducing bandwidth matters, or when you have complex nested data relationships. gRPC uses Protocol Buffers (binary serialization) and HTTP/2, making it extremely fast and efficient — up to 10x smaller payloads and much faster serialization than JSON. It supports bidirectional streaming. The weakness is that it is not human-readable, requires proto file compilation, and is not natively supported in browsers without a proxy. Choose gRPC for internal service-to-service communication in microservices, high-performance systems, or when you need streaming.
hint: REST = simple and compatible. GraphQL = client controls what it gets. gRPC = fastest for internal services but not browser-friendly.

question: What is the difference between HTTP Polling and WebSocket? When should you use each, and how does WebSocket enable real-time communication?
answer: HTTP Polling is a pattern where the client repeatedly sends HTTP requests to the server at regular intervals asking for new data. The server responds to each request with either new data or an empty response. This is simple to implement and works everywhere HTTP works, but it is wasteful: most poll requests return no new data, consuming bandwidth and server resources unnecessarily. It also introduces latency equal to the polling interval — if you poll every 2 seconds and an event happens 0.1 seconds after a poll, you wait 1.9 seconds to learn about it. WebSocket is a persistent, full-duplex communication protocol that starts as an HTTP connection and then upgrades to a WebSocket connection via a handshake. After the handshake, both the client and server can send messages to each other at any time without request-response cycles. The connection stays open for the duration of the session. This enables true real-time communication with minimal latency — when the server has new data it immediately pushes it to the client without waiting for a request. WebSocket is ideal for chat applications, live stock prices, multiplayer games, live dashboards, and collaborative editing. Use HTTP polling when real-time is not critical, when WebSocket infrastructure is not available, or when you need simple compatibility everywhere. Use WebSocket when you need sub-second updates, bidirectional communication, or want to eliminate unnecessary polling traffic.
hint: HTTP polling = client repeatedly asks. WebSocket = persistent connection, server pushes when ready. WebSocket eliminates wasted requests.

question: What is a message queue and what problem does it solve? Explain RabbitMQ's architecture including Producer, Exchange, Queue, Consumer, and the three Exchange types.
answer: A message queue is a system that allows services to communicate asynchronously by sending messages to a queue where consumers can pick them up when ready, rather than calling each other directly and waiting for a response. Message queues solve three critical problems in distributed systems. First, decoupling: the producer does not need to know about or wait for the consumer. If the consumer service is down, messages accumulate in the queue and are processed when it comes back up. Second, load leveling: if a burst of 10,000 orders arrives, the queue absorbs the spike and consumers process them at their own pace rather than crashing the downstream service. Third, fan-out: one event can trigger multiple independent consumers (notification service, inventory service, analytics service) without the producer calling each one. In RabbitMQ, the Producer publishes messages to an Exchange. The Exchange uses routing rules to decide which Queue receives the message. Consumers subscribe to queues and process messages. The three Exchange types are: Direct Exchange routes messages to queues with an exact matching routing key — used for targeted delivery to one specific queue. Fanout Exchange broadcasts to all bound queues ignoring the routing key — used when every consumer needs a copy (like an order created event that multiple services handle). Topic Exchange routes based on pattern matching with wildcards — order.* matches order.created, order.cancelled; *.failed matches payment.failed, inventory.failed.
hint: Exchange = traffic controller. Direct = exact match. Fanout = broadcast all. Topic = wildcard pattern matching.

question: What is idempotency in REST APIs? Which HTTP methods are idempotent and which are not, and how do you make a POST endpoint idempotent using an idempotency key?
answer: An operation is idempotent if performing it multiple times produces the same result as performing it once. In REST APIs, idempotency matters because networks are unreliable — clients may retry a request if they do not receive a response, and if the server already processed the first request and the retry triggers it again, you could end up with duplicate effects. GET is idempotent because reading data multiple times returns the same result and has no side effects. PUT is idempotent because setting a resource to a specific state multiple times leaves it in the same final state — PUT /users/101 with body name:Harsh sets name to Harsh whether called once or ten times. DELETE is idempotent because deleting an already-deleted resource returns 404 but leaves the system in the same state — the resource is absent. POST is NOT idempotent by default because each call creates a new resource — calling POST /payments twice charges the card twice. To make a POST endpoint idempotent, you use an idempotency key: the client generates a unique ID (UUID) for each logical operation and sends it in a header like Idempotency-Key. The server stores this key in a database when processing the request. If the same key arrives again (retry), the server returns the cached response without reprocessing. This is critical for payment APIs where duplicate charges are unacceptable.
hint: Idempotent = safe to retry. GET/PUT/DELETE = yes. POST = no by default. Use idempotency key to make POST safe to retry.

subcategory: Dependency Inversion and Interfaces in Spring
question: How does Spring Boot's dependency injection relate to the Dependency Inversion Principle? What is the difference between using an interface reference vs a concrete class reference when wiring beans?
answer: Spring Boot's dependency injection is a direct implementation of the Dependency Inversion Principle in practice. When you annotate a class with @Service, @Repository, or @Component, Spring creates and manages an instance of it. When another class declares a dependency, Spring injects the right implementation automatically. The power is in using interface references for injected dependencies rather than concrete class references. When you declare a field as RecommendationStrategy strategy (interface type), Spring will inject whatever class implements that interface and is available as a bean. Your class does not know or care which specific implementation it receives — this is DIP in action. If you instead declare the field as TrendingRecommendation strategy (concrete type), your class is tightly coupled to that specific implementation, making it hard to swap, test, or extend. Using interface references means you can easily swap implementations by changing a single @Primary annotation or @Qualifier annotation on the bean, write unit tests by providing a mock implementation of the interface, have different implementations in different environments (production vs test vs development) without changing the consumer class. This is why in Spring applications you almost always see service classes injected as their interface types, not their concrete implementations.
hint: Spring DI = DIP in practice. Inject interfaces not concrete classes. Swap implementations by changing beans, not consumer code.
